title: Find First Non-Repeating Character
description: Given s, return the first character that appears exactly once. If none, return -1.

examples.explanation: Step 1: i=0, char='l'. Inner loop j=0 to 7: j=0 (skip i=j), j=1 char='e' ≠ 'l', j=2 char='e' ≠ 'l', j=3 char='t' ≠ 'l', j=4 char='c' ≠ 'l', j=5 char='o' ≠ 'l', j=6 char='d' ≠ 'l', j=7 char='e' ≠ 'l'. No duplicate found, return 'l'.

Step 1: i=0, char='a'. Inner loop j=0 to 3: j=0 skip, j=1 char='a' equals 'a' → duplicate found, break. Step 2: i=1, char='a'. Inner loop: j=0 char='a' equals 'a' → duplicate, break. Step 3: i=2, char='b'. Inner loop: j=0 char='a' ≠ 'b', j=1 char='a' ≠ 'b', j=2 skip, j=3 char='b' equals 'b' → duplicate, break. Step 4: i=3, char='b'. Inner loop: j=0 char='a' ≠ 'b', j=1 char='a' ≠ 'b', j=2 char='b' equals 'b' → duplicate, break. Loop ends, no non-repeating character found, return -1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each character, check all other characters to see if it repeats. Return the first one with no duplicates. This is straightforward but inefficient for large strings.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Solution for First Non-Repeating Character
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the O(n²) brute force approach to find the first character that appears exactly once in a string.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by checking each character against all other characters to determine if it repeats. The intuition is straightforward: for each character at position i, we examine every other character in the string to see if any match it. If no match is found, that character is non-repeating. We return the first such character encountered. This method is conceptually simple but inefficient for large inputs due to its quadratic time complexity. The algorithm works by iterating through the string with an outer loop (i from 0 to n-1). For each i, an inner loop (j from 0 to n-1) checks if s[i] equals s[j] and i ≠ j. If a duplicate is found, we break and move to the next i. If the inner loop completes without finding a duplicate, we return s[i]. If no such character exists, return -1. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n characters, we potentially check all n characters. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by relying on the key subroutine of checking for duplicates. Common mistakes include not handling the case where i equals j in the inner loop (which would incorrectly flag all characters as duplicates), forgetting to return -1 after the outer loop, and not considering empty strings. The real code pattern involves nested loops with careful index comparison.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, char='l'. Inner loop j=0 to 7: j=0 (skip i=j), j=1 char='e' ≠ 'l', j=2 char='e' ≠ 'l', j=3 char='t' ≠ 'l', j=4 char='c' ≠ 'l', j=5 char='o' ≠ 'l', j=6 char='d' ≠ 'l', j=7 char='e' ≠ 'l'. No duplicate found, return 'l'.

Step 1: i=0, char='a'. Inner loop j=0 to 3: j=0 skip, j=1 char='a' equals 'a' → duplicate found, break. Step 2: i=1, char='a'. Inner loop: j=0 char='a' equals 'a' → duplicate, break. Step 3: i=2, char='b'. Inner loop: j=0 char='a' ≠ 'b', j=1 char='a' ≠ 'b', j=2 skip, j=3 char='b' equals 'b' → duplicate, break. Step 4: i=3, char='b'. Inner loop: j=0 char='a' ≠ 'b', j=1 char='a' ≠ 'b', j=2 char='b' equals 'b' → duplicate, break. Loop ends, no non-repeating character found, return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Checking if a Character is Unique in a String
ladders.desc: Determine if a given character at a specific index appears exactly once in the entire string.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach: verifying whether a character at position i is non-repeating by comparing it with all other characters. The intuition is that a character is unique if no other character in the string matches it, excluding itself. This is achieved by iterating through the string and checking for equality while ensuring we don't compare the character to itself. The algorithm takes a string s and an index i as input. We iterate j from 0 to len(s)-1. If j equals i, we skip to avoid self-comparison. If s[j] equals s[i], we immediately return False (indicating a duplicate). If the loop completes without finding a match, return True. Time complexity is O(n) for each call, as in the worst case we scan the entire string. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each character in the outer loop to identify the first non-repeating one. Common mistakes include forgetting to skip when i equals j, which would cause the function to always return False, and not handling empty strings properly (if s is empty, the function should handle it gracefully, typically by returning False or an error). The real code pattern involves a simple loop with a conditional skip and early return on match.

examples.trace: String: 'hello', i=1, char='e'. j=0: char='h' ≠ 'e'. j=1: skip (i=j). j=2: char='l' ≠ 'e'. j=3: char='l' ≠ 'e'. j=4: char='o' ≠ 'e'. Loop ends with no match found → return true ('e' appears only once).
String: 'apple', i=0, char='a'. j=0: skip. j=1: char='p' ≠ 'a'. j=2: char='p' ≠ 'a'. j=3: char='l' ≠ 'a'. j=4: char='e' ≠ 'a'. No match, return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop Comparison for Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: Using two loops to compare each element against all others to find duplicates.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is the nested loop structure that enables pairwise comparison of characters. The intuition is that to check for duplicates, we need to examine every possible pair of characters in the string. This is done by having an outer loop that selects a character and an inner loop that scans the rest of the string for matches. The key insight is that by iterating i from 0 to n-1 and for each i, iterating j from 0 to n-1, we can compare s[i] with s[j] for all j ≠ i. If s[i] equals s[j] for any j ≠ i, then s[i] is a duplicate. This technique is fundamental to many brute force algorithms and is often the first approach considered for duplicate detection problems. The algorithm involves initializing i=0, then while i < n, set a flag unique=true. Inner loop: j=0 to n-1, if i ≠ j and s[i] equals s[j], set unique=false and break. After inner loop, if unique is true, we have found a non-repeating character. Time complexity is O(n²) due to the nested loops. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as it implements the duplicate check in a more integrated way within the full solution. Common mistakes include not breaking out of the inner loop early when a duplicate is found (inefficient), or incorrectly comparing i and j leading to off-by-one errors. The real code pattern uses nested for-loops with a conditional break.

examples.trace: n=4. i=0, char='a', unique=true. j=0: skip (i=j). j=1: 'b' ≠ 'a'. j=2: 'a' equals 'a' → unique=false, break. i=1, char='b', unique=true. j=0: 'a' ≠ 'b'. j=1: skip. j=2: 'a' ≠ 'b'. j=3: 'c' ≠ 'b'. Loop ends, unique=true, return 'b'.

n=4. i=0, char='x', unique=true. j=0: skip. j=1: 'x' equals 'x' → unique=false, break. i=1, char='x', unique=true. j=0: 'x' equals 'x' → unique=false, break. i=2, char='y', unique=true. j=0: 'x' ≠ 'y'. j=1: 'x' ≠ 'y'. j=2: skip. j=3: 'y' equals 'y' → unique=false, break. i=3, char='y', unique=true. j=0: 'x' ≠ 'y'. j=1: 'x' ≠ 'y'. j=2: 'y' equals 'y' → unique=false, break. Loop ends, no unique found, return -1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through a String with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Looping over a string while keeping track of the current index to access and compare characters.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a string using indices, which is essential for the brute force approach. The intuition is that to compare characters, we need to access each character by its position in the string. This is done using a loop that increments an index variable from 0 to the length of the string minus 1. At each step, we use the index to retrieve the character and perform operations, such as comparisons with other characters. The algorithm starts with i = 0, and while i is less than the length of the string n, we access s[i], then increment i by 1. This allows us to process each character in sequence. Time complexity for a single traversal is O(n), and space complexity is O(1) as we only store the index and maybe the current character. This connects to L2 by providing the mechanism for the outer and inner loops in the nested comparison. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors where the loop goes up to n instead of n-1, leading to index out of bounds, or not updating the index properly. The real code pattern uses a for-loop like 'for i in range(len(s))' or a while-loop with careful incrementing.

examples.output: Characters: h, e, l, l, o

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Characters for Equality
ladders.desc: Checking if two given characters are the same.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to the brute force approach: comparing two characters to see if they are equal. The intuition is simple: in many string algorithms, we need to determine if two characters match, which is a prerequisite for detecting duplicates or patterns. The operation takes two characters as input and returns True if they are identical, False otherwise. In most programming languages, characters are compared using equality operators (e.g., == in Python), which compare their Unicode values or ASCII codes. The algorithm is straightforward: if char1 equals char2, return True; else, return False. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a constant-time comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as it is used within the loop when accessing s[i] and s[j] to check for duplicates. Common mistakes include confusing equality with identity in some languages, or not considering case sensitivity (e.g., 'A' vs 'a' are different). The real code pattern is a simple if-statement: if char1 == char2: return True else return False.

examples.trace: char1='a', char2='a'. They are equal, so return true.
char1='b', char2='c'. They are not equal, so return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Characters in a String by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieving a specific character from a string using its position (index).
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding that a string is a sequence of characters, and each character can be accessed using an index starting from 0. The intuition is that strings are stored as arrays of characters in memory, so we can use an index to pinpoint and retrieve any character. For example, in the string 'hello', index 0 refers to 'h', index 1 to 'e', and so on. The algorithm involves having a string s and an integer index i. If i is within the valid range (0 to len(s)-1), we return s[i]; otherwise, it's an error (e.g., index out of bounds). Time complexity is O(1) for direct access. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L4 as it provides the characters needed for comparison. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (thinking the first character is at index 1) or accessing beyond the string length. The real code pattern is simply using square brackets: s[i] in Python-style.

examples.trace: String: 'world', length=5. Index 1 is valid (0 to 4). s[1] = 'o'.
String: 'cat', length=3. Index 0 is valid. s[0] = 'c'.

approaches.name: HashMap Two-Pass O(n)
approaches.summary: First pass: count frequency of each character using a hashmap. Second pass: iterate through the string to find the first character with count 1. This balances efficiency and simplicity.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is the size of the character set

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find First Non-Repeating Character Using HashMap Two-Pass
ladders.desc: Complete solution that counts character frequencies then finds the first character with count 1.
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the complete problem using the HashMap Two-Pass approach. The intuition is that we need to know both the frequency of each character AND the order in which characters appear. By first counting all frequencies in one pass, we create a complete frequency map. Then in a second pass through the original string order, we can check each character's frequency to find the first one that appears exactly once. This separates the counting logic from the searching logic, making the algorithm clear and efficient. The algorithm works as follows: 1. Initialize an empty hashmap to store character frequencies. 2. First pass: iterate through each character in the string, incrementing its count in the hashmap. 3. Second pass: iterate through the string again, checking each character's frequency in the hashmap. 4. Return the first character with frequency 1, or -1 if none found. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the string length, as we make two passes through the string. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the size of the character set (at most 256 for ASCII). This connects to L1 by implementing the complete solution that uses the frequency counting subroutine. Common mistakes include: checking frequencies during the first pass (which won't work since counts aren't complete), using a data structure that doesn't preserve insertion order, or forgetting to handle the case where no character appears exactly once. Real code pattern: def first_non_repeating(s): freq = {} for char in s: freq[char] = freq.get(char, 0) + 1 for char in s: if freq[char] == 1: return char return -1

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty frequency map {}. Step 2: First pass - count frequencies: l→1, e→3, t→1, c→1, o→1, d→1. Map becomes {'l':1, 'e':3, 't':1, 'c':1, 'o':1, 'd':1}. Step 3: Second pass - check each character in original order: 'l' has frequency 1 → return 'l'.

Step 1: Initialize empty frequency map {}. Step 2: First pass - count frequencies: a→2, b→2, c→2. Map becomes {'a':2, 'b':2, 'c':2}. Step 3: Second pass - check each character: 'a' frequency 2 (not 1), 'a' frequency 2, 'b' frequency 2, 'b' frequency 2, 'c' frequency 2, 'c' frequency 2. Step 4: No character with frequency 1 found → return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Count Character Frequencies Using HashMap
ladders.desc: Create a frequency map counting occurrences of each character in a string.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the most important helper function in the full solution. The intuition is that we need to know how many times each character appears before we can determine which ones appear exactly once. By building a complete frequency map first, we separate the counting logic from the searching logic. The algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty hashmap. 2. Iterate through each character in the string. 3. For each character, check if it exists in the hashmap. 4. If it exists, increment its count by 1. If it doesn't exist, add it to the hashmap with count 1. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the string length, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the number of unique characters. This connects to L0 by providing the frequency data needed for the second pass. Common mistakes include: using an array without considering character encoding, forgetting to initialize counts properly, or using inefficient data structures. Real code pattern: def count_frequencies(s): freq = {} for char in s: freq[char] = freq.get(char, 0) + 1 return freq

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty map {}. Step 2: Process 'h' → not in map → add {'h':1}. Step 3: Process 'e' → not in map → add {'h':1, 'e':1}. Step 4: Process 'l' → not in map → add {'h':1, 'e':1, 'l':1}. Step 5: Process 'l' → already in map → increment to {'h':1, 'e':1, 'l':2}. Step 6: Process 'o' → not in map → add {'h':1, 'e':1, 'l':2, 'o':1}.

Step 1: Initialize empty map {}. Step 2: Process 'a' → not in map → add {'a':1}. Step 3: Process 'a' → already in map → increment to {'a':2}. Step 4: Process 'a' → already in map → increment to {'a':3}. Step 5: Process 'b' → not in map → add {'a':3, 'b':1}. Step 6: Process 'b' → already in map → increment to {'a':3, 'b':2}. Step 7: Process 'b' → already in map → increment to {'a':3, 'b':3}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find First Element with Specific Property While Preserving Order
ladders.desc: Iterate through a sequence to find the first element meeting a condition, using pre-computed data.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of the approach: we need to find the FIRST element with a specific property (frequency = 1), which requires preserving the original order while checking against pre-computed data. The intuition is that order matters for 'first', so we must iterate through the original sequence, not the frequency map. The algorithm: 1. Have pre-computed data about each element (like frequency counts). 2. Iterate through the original sequence in order. 3. For each element, check if it meets the condition using the pre-computed data. 4. Return the first element that meets the condition, or a sentinel value if none do. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration. Space complexity is O(1) additional space beyond the pre-computed data. This connects to L1 by using the frequency map as the pre-computed data. Common mistakes include: iterating through the frequency map (which loses order), checking conditions during the counting phase, or using inefficient search methods. Real code pattern: def find_first_with_property(sequence, property_data): for element in sequence: if property_data[element] meets condition: return element return sentinel_value

examples.trace: Step 1: Iterate through original sequence ['a','b','c','a','b']. Step 2: Check 'a' → property_data['a'] = 2 (not 1). Step 3: Check 'b' → property_data['b'] = 2 (not 1). Step 4: Check 'c' → property_data['c'] = 1 (meets condition) → return 'c'.

Step 1: Iterate through original sequence [1,2,3,1,2,3]. Step 2: Check 1 → property_data[1] = 2 (not 1). Step 3: Check 2 → property_data[2] = 2 (not 1). Step 4: Check 3 → property_data[3] = 2 (not 1). Step 5: Check 1 → property_data[1] = 2 (not 1). Step 6: Check 2 → property_data[2] = 2 (not 1). Step 7: Check 3 → property_data[3] = 2 (not 1). Step 8: No element meets condition → return -1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through String and Access HashMap
ladders.desc: Basic string iteration combined with hashmap lookup operations.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept combines two fundamental operations: iterating through a string character by character, and looking up/updating values in a hashmap. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters that we can process sequentially, while hashmaps allow efficient O(1) access to stored data. The algorithm: 1. Initialize a hashmap. 2. Use a loop to iterate through each character in the string. 3. For each character, check if it exists in the hashmap. 4. If it exists, retrieve its current value, increment it, and store back. If it doesn't exist, add it with an initial value. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration with O(1) hashmap operations. Space complexity is O(k) for the hashmap storage. This connects to L2 by providing the mechanism to build the property data while preserving order through iteration. Common mistakes include: modifying the string during iteration, using wrong hashmap methods, or not handling missing keys properly. Real code pattern: for char in s: if char in hashmap: hashmap[char] += 1 else: hashmap[char] = 1

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty hashmap {}. Step 2: First iteration - char='c': 'c' not in hashmap → hashmap={'c':1}. Step 3: Second iteration - char='a': 'a' not in hashmap → hashmap={'c':1, 'a':1}. Step 4: Third iteration - char='t': 't' not in hashmap → hashmap={'c':1, 'a':1, 't':1}.

Step 1: Initialize empty hashmap {}. Step 2: First iteration - char='s': 's' not in hashmap → hashmap={'s':1}. Step 3: Second iteration - char='e': 'e' not in hashmap → hashmap={'s':1, 'e':1}. Step 4: Third iteration - char='e': 'e' is in hashmap → current value 1 → increment to 2 → hashmap={'s':1, 'e':2}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — HashMap Insertion and Update
ladders.desc: Fundamental hashmap operations: checking existence, inserting new keys, and updating existing values.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves the basic hashmap manipulations needed for counting. The intuition is that hashmaps store key-value pairs and allow efficient O(1) average time operations. We need three operations: 1. Check if a key exists. 2. If it doesn't exist, insert it with an initial value. 3. If it does exist, retrieve the current value, modify it, and store it back. The algorithm for incrementing a count: 1. Check if key exists in hashmap. 2. If not, add key with value 1. 3. If yes, get current value, add 1 to it, and store back. Time complexity is O(1) average for each operation. Space complexity is O(1) per unique key. This connects to L3 by providing the specific hashmap operations used during string iteration. Common mistakes include: not checking existence before access (causing KeyError), using wrong update patterns, or inefficient approaches. Real code pattern: if key in hashmap: hashmap[key] += 1 else: hashmap[key] = 1

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if 'a' exists in hashmap → yes. Step 2: Get current value: hashmap['a'] = 2. Step 3: Increment: 2 + 1 = 3. Step 4: Store back: hashmap['a'] = 3. Result: {'a':3}.
Step 1: Check if 'b' exists in hashmap → no. Step 2: Since key doesn't exist, add it with initial value 1. Step 3: Result: {'a':1, 'b':1}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Iterate Through String Characters
ladders.desc: Basic string traversal: accessing each character in sequence.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: how to iterate through a string to access each character one by one. The intuition is that a string is a sequence of characters, and we can process them sequentially using a loop. This foundational skill is essential because the entire algorithm depends on examining each character. The algorithm: 1. Start at the beginning of the string. 2. Get the first character. 3. Process that character (do something with it). 4. Move to the next character. 5. Repeat until all characters are processed. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the string length. Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration itself. This connects to L4 by providing the sequence of characters that will be used as keys in the hashmap operations. Common mistakes include: trying to modify strings during iteration (strings are immutable), using wrong iteration methods, or off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: for char in s: # process char

examples.trace: Step 1: Start iteration. Step 2: First character: 'a'. Step 3: Process 'a'. Step 4: Next character: 'b'. Step 5: Process 'b'. Step 6: Next character: 'c'. Step 7: Process 'c'. Step 8: No more characters → done.

examples.output: Characters: h, e, l, l, o
examples.trace: Step 1: Start iteration. Step 2: First character: 'h'. Step 3: Process 'h'. Step 4: Next character: 'e'. Step 5: Process 'e'. Step 6: Next character: 'l'. Step 7: Process 'l'. Step 8: Next character: 'l'. Step 9: Process 'l'. Step 10: Next character: 'o'. Step 11: Process 'o'. Step 12: No more characters → done.

testCases.expected: Characters: p, y, t, h, o, n

approaches.name: Array Two-Pass O(n)
approaches.summary: Similar to hashmap two-pass, but uses a fixed-size array (e.g., size 256 for ASCII) to store frequencies. This is optimal for known character sets like ASCII.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) due to fixed array size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find First Non-Repeating Character Using Array Two-Pass
ladders.desc: Complete solution using fixed-size array to track character frequencies and find first unique character.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by leveraging a fixed-size array to count character frequencies in two passes through the string. The intuition is that we need to identify characters that appear exactly once, and among those, find the first one in the original string order. Using a fixed array (size 256 for ASCII) gives us O(1) space complexity since the array size doesn't scale with input size. In the first pass, we count occurrences of each character by converting characters to their ASCII values and using those as indices into the array. In the second pass, we check each character's frequency in the array and return the first one with count 1. If no such character exists, we return -1. The time complexity is O(n) for two passes through the string, and space is O(1) due to the fixed array. This approach is optimal for ASCII strings and avoids hashmap overhead. Connection to L1: This builds on the frequency counting subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty strings, not converting characters properly to array indices, or returning the character instead of -1 when none are unique. Real code pattern: Initialize array of size 256 with zeros, first pass increment counts, second pass check counts and return first with count 1, else return -1.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize freq array of size 256 with all zeros.
Step 2: First pass through 'leetcode':
  'l' (ASCII 108): freq[108] = 1
  'e' (ASCII 101): freq[101] = 1
  'e' (ASCII 101): freq[101] = 2
  't' (ASCII 116): freq[116] = 1
  'c' (ASCII 99): freq[99] = 1
  'o' (ASCII 111): freq[111] = 1
  'd' (ASCII 100): freq[100] = 1
  'e' (ASCII 101): freq[101] = 3
Step 3: Second pass through 'leetcode':
  'l' (ASCII 108): freq[108] = 1 → return 'l'
Final output: 'l'

Step 1: Initialize freq array of size 256 with all zeros.
Step 2: First pass through 'aabbcc':
  'a' (ASCII 97): freq[97] = 1
  'a' (ASCII 97): freq[97] = 2
  'b' (ASCII 98): freq[98] = 1
  'b' (ASCII 98): freq[98] = 2
  'c' (ASCII 99): freq[99] = 1
  'c' (ASCII 99): freq[99] = 2
Step 3: Second pass through 'aabbcc':
  'a' (ASCII 97): freq[97] = 2 (not 1)
  'a' (ASCII 97): freq[97] = 2
  'b' (ASCII 98): freq[98] = 2
  'b' (ASCII 98): freq[98] = 2
  'c' (ASCII 99): freq[99] = 2
  'c' (ASCII 99): freq[99] = 2
Step 4: No character has freq = 1 → return -1
Final output: -1

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Count Character Frequencies Using Fixed Array
ladders.desc: Build frequency array by counting occurrences of each character in a string.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the full solution: counting how many times each character appears in the input string. The intuition is that we need to know character frequencies to identify unique characters. We use a fixed-size array (size 256 for ASCII) where each index corresponds to a character's ASCII value. By iterating through the string once, we increment the count at the appropriate index for each character. This gives us O(n) time complexity for the pass and O(1) space for the array. Connection to L0: This frequency array is used in the second pass of the full solution to find the first unique character. Common mistakes include not initializing the array to zeros, using wrong array size, or not handling non-ASCII characters if required. Real code pattern: Create array of 256 zeros, loop through string, convert each char to ASCII (ord in Python), increment array at that index.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize freq array of size 256 with all zeros.
Step 2: Process 'hello':
  'h' (ASCII 104): freq[104] = 0 → 1
  'e' (ASCII 101): freq[101] = 0 → 1
  'l' (ASCII 108): freq[108] = 0 → 1
  'l' (ASCII 108): freq[108] = 1 → 2
  'o' (ASCII 111): freq[111] = 0 → 1
Result: freq[104]=1, freq[101]=1, freq[108]=2, freq[111]=1, others 0

Step 1: Initialize freq array of size 256 with all zeros.
Step 2: Process 'aaabbb':
  'a' (ASCII 97): freq[97] = 0 → 1
  'a' (ASCII 97): freq[97] = 1 → 2
  'a' (ASCII 97): freq[97] = 2 → 3
  'b' (ASCII 98): freq[98] = 0 → 1
  'b' (ASCII 98): freq[98] = 1 → 2
  'b' (ASCII 98): freq[98] = 2 → 3
Result: freq[97]=3, freq[98]=3, others 0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identify First Character with Frequency 1
ladders.desc: Scan string to find first character whose frequency count equals 1.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: after counting frequencies, we need to find the first character in the original string order that appears exactly once. The intuition is that order matters—we can't just find any unique character; it must be the first one. We iterate through the string a second time, checking each character's frequency in the pre-computed array. The first character with frequency 1 is our answer. If none are found, we return -1. Time complexity is O(n) for the scan, and space is O(1) for the array. Connection to L1: This uses the frequency array built in the previous step. Common mistakes include scanning the array instead of the string (which loses order), or not handling the -1 case properly. Real code pattern: Loop through string, get ASCII value, check if freq[value] == 1, return char if true, else return -1 after loop.

examples.input: String: 'leetcode', Freq: l=1, e=3, t=1, c=1, o=1, d=1
examples.trace:
Step 1: Given freq array with counts: l(108)=1, e(101)=3, t(116)=1, c(99)=1, o(111)=1, d(100)=1.
Step 2: Scan 'leetcode':
  'l' (108): freq[108] = 1 → return 'l'
Final output: 'l'

Step 1: Given freq array with counts: a(97)=2, b(98)=2.
Step 2: Scan 'aabb':
  'a' (97): freq[97] = 2 (not 1)
  'a' (97): freq[97] = 2
  'b' (98): freq[98] = 2
  'b' (98): freq[98] = 2
Step 3: No character has freq = 1 → return -1
Final output: -1

testCases.input: String: 'hello', Freq: h=1, e=1, l=2, o=1

ladders.title: Building Block — Convert Characters to Array Indices Using ASCII
ladders.desc: Map each character to its ASCII value for use as an index in fixed array.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for using a fixed-size array: we need to convert characters to numerical indices. For ASCII characters, each has a unique code from 0-127 (standard ASCII) or 0-255 (extended ASCII). We use these codes as direct indices into our array. The intuition is that arrays are indexed by integers, so we transform characters to their integer ASCII values. This gives O(1) access to frequency counts. Time and space are negligible for the conversion. Connection to L2: This enables checking frequencies in the array during the scan. Common mistakes include assuming all characters are lowercase letters, not handling uppercase or digits, or using wrong ASCII ranges. Real code pattern: Use ord(char) in Python to get ASCII value, use as index in array.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Character 'a' is a lowercase letter.
Step 2: In ASCII, 'a' has decimal value 97.
Step 3: Use 97 as index in freq array.
Result: Index 97

Step 1: Character 'Z' is an uppercase letter.
Step 2: In ASCII, 'Z' has decimal value 90.
Step 3: Use 90 as index in freq array.
Result: Index 90

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Increment Array Element at Index
ladders.desc: Increase the value stored at a specific array index by 1.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used to count frequencies: for each character, we increment the count at its corresponding array index. The intuition is simple: we're tallying occurrences. In code, this is typically done with array[index] += 1. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, and space is for the array. Connection to L3: After converting a character to an index, we perform this increment. Common mistakes include using wrong index, not initializing array to zero first, or incrementing by wrong amount. Real code pattern: freq[ord(char)] += 1.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with array [0,0,0].
Step 2: Index is 1.
Step 3: Increment value at index 1: array[1] = 0 → 1.
Result: Array becomes [0,1,0]

Step 1: Start with array [2,3,4].
Step 2: Index is 0.
Step 3: Increment value at index 0: array[0] = 2 → 3.
Result: Array becomes [3,3,4]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access Array Element by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve or modify the value at a specific position in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how arrays work. An array is a data structure that stores elements at indices (positions), starting from 0. We can access an element using its index, e.g., array[0] gives the first element. The intuition is that arrays provide direct, O(1) access to any element if you know its index. This is fundamental for counting frequencies efficiently. Time complexity is O(1) for access, space is for the array size. Connection to L4: This operation is used to read and update counts. Common mistakes include using indices out of bounds, confusing 0-based indexing, or not understanding that indices are integers. Real code pattern: value = array[index] to read, array[index] = value to write.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Array has elements: index 0 = 10, index 1 = 20, index 2 = 30.
Step 2: Access element at index 1.
Step 3: Value is 20.
Result: 20

Step 1: Array has elements: index 0 = 5, index 1 = 6, index 2 = 7.
Step 2: Access element at index 0.
Step 3: Value is 5.
Result: 5
